q: What Spark workspace settings can you configure in Microsoft Fabric, and where do you find them?
a: Spark workspace settings in Microsoft Fabric are located under Workspace Settings → Data Engineering/Science → Spark Settings. From there you can select the default environment (which bundles libraries, Spark configuration, and resource settings), configure the default pool (starter pool or a custom pool), enable or disable High Concurrency mode so multiple notebook sessions share one running pool, set autoscale and dynamic executor allocation options, and define Spark properties that apply to every session in the workspace. The starter pool starts sessions in roughly 10–15 seconds by keeping warm executors pre-allocated; custom pools let you choose node size, min/max nodes, and idle timeout. Capacity admins must first enable 'Customized workspace pools' in the capacity admin settings before workspace owners can create custom pools. Runtime version is also set per environment, not globally, because different teams may need different Spark or Python versions.
fj: Spark pool settings are configured per notebook using the `%%configure` magic command at the cell level and cannot be set at the workspace level.
fs: While `%%configure` can override session-level Spark properties in a single notebook, workspace-level defaults (default pool, autoscale, High Concurrency mode, default environment) are set in Workspace Settings → Spark Settings and apply to every new session unless overridden. Using only `%%configure` skips the central governance controls.

code.label: Override Spark config per notebook session using %%configure
lines: # Run this in the FIRST cell of a notebook to override workspace defaults
# %%configure only applies to the current session — workspace defaults are unchanged

q: How do you configure OneLake workspace settings, and what is the difference between OneLake security and item-level permissions?
a: OneLake workspace settings are accessed via Workspace Settings → OneLake. The key toggle is 'OneLake security', which enables folder-level access control on OneLake itself—meaning you can restrict which users can read specific folders within a Lakehouse Files or Tables section without granting them the full item-level ReadAll permission. Without OneLake security enabled, every workspace Member or higher can read all data in every item in that workspace. OneLake security is additive with item-level permissions: item-level permissions (Read, ReadAll, Write) are set via the Share dialog on the Fabric item, while OneLake security provides sub-item folder grants for fine-grained access. Workspace Admins, Members, and Contributors bypass OneLake security and always have full read-write regardless. OneLake security as of early 2026 is in preview and supports Lakehouses, Warehouses via their SQL analytics endpoint, and semantic models in DirectLake mode. You configure folder-level grants in the Lakehouse settings or via the OneLake File Explorer after security is enabled.
fj: Setting OneLake security on a workspace restricts workspace Members from reading any data unless explicitly granted, overriding workspace role permissions.
fs: OneLake security only affects Viewers and users who access data through item-level Read/ReadAll grants. Workspace Admins, Members, and Contributors retain full access and are never restricted by OneLake folder-level grants. Restricting Members requires removing them from the workspace role.

code.label: Grant OneLake folder access via Fabric REST API (data access role)
lines: # Fabric REST API: assign a user to a OneLake data access role on a Lakehouse

q: What are Dataflow Gen2 workspace settings in Microsoft Fabric, and how does the staging Lakehouse affect behavior?
a: Dataflow Gen2 workspace settings are found under Workspace Settings → Data Factory → Dataflow Gen2. The main options are: (1) Staging Lakehouse — a workspace-level Lakehouse used as an intermediate buffer during query evaluation; when enabled, Fabric materializes data at the query folding boundary so that subsequent steps can process large volumes in Spark rather than loading everything into memory; (2) Staging Warehouse — an alternative staging destination for T-SQL-based evaluation; (3) Fast Copy — a setting that enables the high-throughput COPY INTO path for supported sources, trading some transformation flexibility for much higher ingestion speed. The staging Lakehouse is automatically created and managed by Fabric in a system workspace; you should not manually edit its contents. Disabling staging forces all transformations into the M engine in-memory, which is fine for small tables but fails or slows significantly on millions of rows. Each refresh creates and cleans up staging artifacts automatically.
fj: Disabling the staging Lakehouse in Dataflow Gen2 settings is a best practice for performance because it eliminates the overhead of writing intermediate data to storage.
fs: Disabling staging forces all processing into the in-memory M engine, which is only appropriate for small datasets. For large or complex transformations, the staging Lakehouse enables query folding to Spark, dramatically improving performance and avoiding out-of-memory errors.

q: How do you connect a Microsoft Fabric workspace to a Git repository for version control, and what item types are supported?
a: Git integration is configured under Workspace Settings → Git integration. You connect to either Azure DevOps (Azure Repos) or GitHub by supplying the organization, project, repository, branch, and root folder. Once connected, the workspace synchronizes items to the specified branch: each item is serialized as a folder containing definition files (e.g., notebook `.ipynb`, pipeline `.json`, Dataflow Gen2 `.json`, Lakehouse metadata). Supported item types as of 2026 include notebooks, Spark job definitions, pipelines, Dataflow Gen2, semantic models (PBIR format), reports, lakehouses (metadata only, not data files), warehouses (schema definition only), ML models, ML experiments, and eventstreams. Note that data stored in a Lakehouse—Delta tables and Files—is not version-controlled; only the item configuration is. The recommended workflow is one branch per developer: each person connects their dev workspace to a feature branch, commits changes, raises a pull request, and a staging workspace connected to the main branch is updated after merge. The new Fabric CLI `deploy` command (March 2026) enables automated workspace deployment from CI pipelines without custom scripting.
fj: Fabric Git integration version-controls all data in a Lakehouse, including the Parquet files and Delta logs stored in OneLake.
fs: Fabric Git integration tracks item metadata and configuration only—notebook code, pipeline JSON, schema DDL—not the actual data files stored in OneLake. Delta table data must be managed separately with backup strategies like CLONE or COPY INTO.

q: What are database projects in Microsoft Fabric, and how do they support CI/CD for warehouse development?
a: A database project in Microsoft Fabric is a source-controlled representation of a Fabric Warehouse schema as a set of T-SQL object definition files (.sql), analogous to SSDT projects in traditional SQL Server development. You can create a database project directly from the Fabric Warehouse editor, which exports all tables, views, stored procedures, and functions as individual SQL scripts. These scripts are stored in Git via the workspace Git integration, enabling code review workflows for schema changes. When you want to promote changes, you run the database project deployment against the target workspace's Warehouse using the SqlPackage tool or Fabric deployment pipelines, which compares the project state to the live database and applies an incremental diff—adding new objects and altering changed ones without dropping unrelated objects. Database projects are particularly useful for multi-environment promotion (dev → test → prod) because deployment rules in deployment pipelines can swap connection strings per stage.
fj: Database projects in Fabric are used to export and import data snapshots from a Warehouse for backup and restore purposes.
fs: Database projects manage schema definitions (DDL), not data. They enable CI/CD for warehouse object changes—tables, views, procedures—not data backup. For data movement between environments use Copy Activity or CTAS patterns.

code.label: Example: stored procedure tracked in database project
lines: -- This file is committed to Git as part of the database project.

q: How do you create and configure deployment pipelines in Microsoft Fabric, and what are deployment rules used for?
a: Deployment pipelines are created from the Deployment Pipelines section in the Fabric portal. A pipeline has up to three stages—Development, Test, Production—each mapped to a Fabric workspace. You assign workspaces to stages during setup. To promote content you click 'Deploy' between adjacent stages, which copies all or selected items from the source workspace to the target. Deployment rules are per-item overrides applied when content lands in a stage: for example, a semantic model dataset rule can point to a different data source server, a pipeline rule can swap a Lakehouse connection to the production Lakehouse instead of the development one, and a Dataflow rule can change credentials or staging settings. This means you deploy once and the pipeline automatically adjusts environment-specific config. The Variable Libraries feature announced at FabCon 2026 extends this by letting you define a key-value store per workspace that items reference at runtime, eliminating hard-coded IDs across stages. Access to deployment pipelines requires the workspace Admin or Member role in both source and target workspaces.
fj: Deployment pipelines copy workspace content including all Delta table data from the source Lakehouse to the target Lakehouse during a deploy operation.
fs: Deployment pipelines copy item configurations (notebook code, pipeline JSON, semantic model metadata) but do not copy data. Delta tables and Files stored in OneLake remain in the source workspace's storage. Data promotion requires separate processes such as running a pipeline that writes to the target environment's Lakehouse.

q: What are the workspace roles in Microsoft Fabric, and what access does each grant to Fabric items?
a: Microsoft Fabric workspaces have four roles: Admin, Member, Contributor, and Viewer. Admin can manage all workspace settings, add/remove members at any role level, delete the workspace, and modify Git integration and deployment pipeline assignments. Member can add Members and Contributors, create and edit all item types, manage data connections, and publish apps. Contributor can create and edit items but cannot manage workspace membership or settings. Viewer has read-only access to items using the item's built-in viewer (e.g., report view, notebook read mode) but cannot access the underlying data through the SQL analytics endpoint or OneLake unless granted explicit item-level permissions. For sharing data beyond workspace membership you use the item Share dialog, which grants Read (view via app/report), ReadAll (direct data access via SQL analytics endpoint and OneLake APIs), or Build permission (build new content on top of the semantic model). Workspace roles are additive with item-level permissions: a Viewer with a ReadAll item grant can query the underlying Lakehouse tables via the SQL analytics endpoint.
fj: A Fabric workspace Viewer can query Lakehouse tables via the SQL analytics endpoint by default because they have read access to the workspace.
fs: Workspace Viewer only grants access to the item's front-end experience (e.g., reading a report). To query underlying data through the SQL analytics endpoint or OneLake APIs, a Viewer needs an explicit ReadAll or SQL-level grant on the specific item.

q: How do you implement item-level access controls in Microsoft Fabric, and how does sharing differ from workspace roles?
a: Item-level access controls in Fabric are applied through the Share dialog on an individual item (right-click → Share, or the '…' menu). You can share with specific users or groups and choose permission tiers: Read (view the item in its UI context, e.g., a report), ReadAll (access the item's underlying data via SQL analytics endpoint and OneLake), Build (create derived content from a semantic model), and Write (edit the item). Item-level sharing is the primary mechanism for giving external users or service principals access to a single artifact without adding them to the workspace. For Lakehouses and Warehouses, sharing with ReadAll lets the recipient run T-SQL queries against the SQL analytics endpoint using tools like Azure Data Studio, SSMS, or a JDBC connection. For Power BI semantic models, the Build permission allows recipients to create reports in their own workspace using the shared model. A key nuance: sharing does not grant OneLake folder-level access unless OneLake security folder grants are also configured—ReadAll grants access to the entire item, not individual folders within it.
fj: Sharing a Lakehouse with 'Read' permission allows the recipient to query all Delta tables using T-SQL or the OneLake API.
fs: 'Read' permission only allows viewing the Lakehouse item in the Fabric UI. To query tables via the SQL analytics endpoint or access OneLake files via API, the recipient needs the 'ReadAll' permission (or 'ReadData' in some contexts). 'Read' alone does not expose the data layer.

q: How does dynamic data masking work in Microsoft Fabric Warehouse, and how do you configure a masking rule?
a: Dynamic data masking (DDM) in Microsoft Fabric Warehouse works the same as in Azure Synapse and SQL Server: when a user queries a masked column, the database engine returns a masked substitute value instead of the real data—without altering the stored data. Masking rules are defined per column using T-SQL DDL. Four built-in mask functions exist: `default()` replaces the full value with a function-specific placeholder (e.g., `XXXX` for strings, `0` for numbers, `1900-01-01` for dates); `partial(prefix, padding, suffix)` exposes a prefix and suffix of a string; `email()` shows the first character and the domain placeholder; `random(low, high)` returns a random number in a range for numeric columns. Users with the `UNMASK` permission bypass DDM and see real values—this permission should be granted only to privileged roles. DDM does not protect data at rest or in transit; it only filters results at query time. It also does not prevent inference attacks from WHERE clauses—a masked user can still filter `WHERE email = 'alice@contoso.com'` and infer whether that record exists.
fj: Dynamic data masking encrypts sensitive columns in the Fabric Warehouse so that unauthorized users cannot read the raw bytes even with direct storage access.
fs: DDM is a query-time display control only—it masks values returned in result sets. The underlying data in storage is not encrypted by DDM. Storage encryption is handled separately by Microsoft-managed or customer-managed keys at the Azure Storage layer.

code.label: Define DDM on email and SSN columns
lines: -- Apply masking on an existing table
-- Grant unmask only to privileged role
-- A normal user sees: a***@contoso.com, XXX-XX-6789

q: How do you apply Microsoft Purview sensitivity labels to Microsoft Fabric items, and what downstream effects do they have?
a: Sensitivity labels from Microsoft Purview Information Protection can be applied to Fabric items (Lakehouses, Warehouses, semantic models, reports, dataflows, notebooks, and others) through the item's '…' menu → Sensitivity label, or programmatically via the Fabric REST APIs. Labels are inherited: when you export data from a labeled item—such as downloading a report to PDF or connecting a semantic model to Excel—the label propagates to the exported file, triggering any DLP policies attached to that label classification. Labels are also reflected in the Purview Data Catalog, enabling governance teams to discover sensitive data across the tenant without querying actual content. A tenant admin must enable sensitivity labels for Fabric in the Fabric Admin Portal under Tenant Settings → Information Protection. If 'Mandatory labeling' is turned on, users must assign a label before saving any new item. DLP policies in Purview can restrict access to semantic models or warehouses that contain labels above a configured threshold—for example, blocking external sharing of anything labeled 'Highly Confidential'.
fj: Sensitivity labels in Fabric encrypt the underlying Delta Parquet files in OneLake so that only users with matching label clearance can read them.
fs: Sensitivity labels in Fabric are metadata classifiers, not encryption mechanisms for OneLake storage. They influence export behavior, DLP policy enforcement, and catalog discovery. File-level encryption in OneLake is handled by Azure Storage encryption (AES-256), not sensitivity labels.

q: How do you implement and use Microsoft Fabric audit logs to investigate user activity?
a: Microsoft Fabric user activities are logged to the Microsoft Purview audit log. To access them, go to the Microsoft Purview compliance portal → Audit → New search, select the date range, and filter by 'Workloads' → 'PowerBI' (Fabric activities appear under the Power BI workload umbrella). Fabric-specific activities include FabricItemCreated, FabricItemDeleted, FabricItemUpdated, GetLakehouse, RunNotebook, ExportDataflowQuery, and around 100 others. Audit log entries contain the user or service principal identity, the activity type, the Fabric item name and ID, the workspace, the timestamp, and request metadata. Logs are retained for 90 days by default (up to 1 year with an E5 or Purview compliance add-on). For programmatic access and custom dashboards, export logs using the Activity Events REST API (`GET /v1.0/myorg/admin/activityevents`) or the PowerShell `Get-PowerBIActivityEvent` cmdlet, then pipe into a Lakehouse for long-term storage and KQL or SQL analysis. Audit logging must be enabled in the Fabric Admin Portal → Tenant Settings → Audit logs.
fj: Fabric audit logs are stored in OneLake automatically and can be queried using KQL from an Eventhouse in the same tenant.
fs: Fabric audit logs are stored in the Microsoft Purview audit log service, not in a user's OneLake. To bring them into OneLake for long-term analysis you must export them via the Activity Events API and load them with a pipeline or notebook—they are not natively queryable via KQL without that export step.

code.label: Export Fabric activity events via REST API
lines: # Use a service principal with Power BI Admin scope
# Write to Lakehouse Files for further analysis

q: How do you configure and implement OneLake security for fine-grained folder access control?
a: OneLake security is enabled per workspace under Workspace Settings → OneLake → OneLake security: On. Once enabled, you navigate to the target Lakehouse, open its settings, and manage 'Data access roles'—named role definitions that grant Read access to specific Tables or Files subfolders within that Lakehouse. You assign users or groups to these roles. OneLake security is enforced when users access data via OneLake APIs, the SQL analytics endpoint (T-SQL), ADLS-compatible API, and Fabric Spark in the same workspace. A critical design constraint: OneLake security only restricts Viewers and users with item-level Read/ReadAll grants. Workspace Admins, Members, and Contributors bypass folder-level security entirely and see all data. For a given shortcut pointing to an external OneLake path, security is evaluated at the source item—the shortcut itself cannot override source permissions. Best practice is to use OneLake security together with workspace roles: give BI consumers Viewer access plus restricted OneLake folder grants, keeping the workspace-role footprint minimal.
fj: OneLake security data access roles restrict all users including workspace Admins and Members from accessing folders they have not been explicitly granted.
fs: OneLake security only restricts Viewers and users accessing items through item-level Read/ReadAll permissions. Workspace Admins, Members, and Contributors always have full read-write access to all OneLake data regardless of folder-level grants, so OneLake security cannot substitute for workspace role governance.

q: When should you choose Dataflow Gen2 vs. a pipeline vs. a notebook for orchestrating data movement in Microsoft Fabric?
a: The right tool depends on the transformation complexity, data volume, and team skillset. Dataflow Gen2 (Power Query) is best for structured source extraction and light-to-medium transformations that benefit from query folding—pushing work back to the source—and for teams comfortable with a no-code/low-code UI. It handles hundreds of source connectors, incremental refresh, and outputs directly to Lakehouse or Warehouse tables. Pipelines (Data Factory) are the orchestration layer: use them when you need to sequence multiple activities (Copy, Dataflow, Notebook, Stored Procedure), implement conditional branching, handle retry logic, parameterize runs with dynamic expressions, or trigger cross-workspace operations. Pipelines do not transform data themselves—they call other engines. Notebooks with PySpark or Spark SQL are the choice for large-volume complex transformations, machine learning preprocessing, custom parsing, or anything exceeding Power Query's in-memory limits. A common production pattern: a pipeline that calls a Dataflow Gen2 for normalized extraction into Silver tables, then calls a Notebook for business-logic Gold aggregations, with error handling between steps.
fj: A pipeline is the best tool for transforming large volumes of data with complex business logic because it is the most powerful compute engine in Fabric.
fs: Pipelines are orchestrators, not compute engines—they have no built-in data transformation capability beyond the Copy activity's basic column mapping. For heavy transformation, pipelines invoke Notebooks (Spark) or Dataflow Gen2 (Power Query). Choosing a pipeline alone for complex transformation would result in writing all logic in a Copy Activity mapping expression, which is brittle and limited.

q: How do you design event-based triggers and scheduled triggers in Microsoft Fabric pipelines?
a: Microsoft Fabric Data Factory pipelines support two trigger types. Scheduled triggers define a recurrence pattern—specific times, daily, weekly, or a cron expression—and are configured in the pipeline editor under 'Schedule'. You can set start/end dates, time zone, and recurrence. Event-based triggers fire a pipeline when a file arrives in or is deleted from a Fabric OneLake path (Storage event trigger) or when a custom Fabric Activator reflex fires. The OneLake storage event trigger monitors a specified Lakehouse Files folder using Azure Event Grid under the hood; you configure a blob path filter (prefix and suffix) to narrow which file events activate the pipeline. For multi-pipeline orchestration you can also use the Pipeline activity inside a parent pipeline to call a child pipeline synchronously or asynchronously, passing parameters. Event-based triggers are stateless—each file arrival fires an independent pipeline run, so if files arrive faster than pipelines complete, multiple concurrent runs are launched. To prevent that, configure the trigger concurrency limit or use a queue-based pattern where the trigger writes a message and a single consumer pipeline processes one item at a time.
fj: Fabric event-based triggers can monitor any Azure Blob Storage or ADLS Gen2 container external to the workspace to fire a pipeline.
fs: In Microsoft Fabric, event-based storage triggers natively monitor OneLake paths within the workspace. Monitoring external ADLS Gen2 accounts requires Azure Data Factory or a custom solution using Event Grid subscriptions that call the Fabric pipeline via a webhook or Logic App.

q: How do you implement parameterized orchestration patterns with notebooks and pipelines in Microsoft Fabric?
a: Parameterization in Fabric pipelines uses pipeline parameters defined at the pipeline level and passed to activities via dynamic expressions using the `@pipeline().parameters.<name>` syntax or `@activity('<name>').output` for chaining outputs. When invoking a Notebook activity from a pipeline, you pass base parameters in the activity's 'Settings' tab—these map to the cell tagged `parameters` in the notebook (using the `parameters` tag in the cell metadata). Inside the notebook, parameter values from the pipeline override the tagged-cell variables directly; you can also read them programmatically using `notebookutils.notebook.entry_point` if needed. For dynamic expressions in pipeline activities—such as a file path containing today's date—use the expression language: `@concat('/raw/orders/', formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'yyyy/MM/dd'))`. For-Each activities loop over an array parameter, spinning child activities per item. Copy and Dataflow activities accept dataset parameters so a single pipeline definition handles multiple table names. Best practice: define all environment-specific values (workspace IDs, Lakehouse names) in a Variable Library or pipeline parameter set rather than hardcoding them, so deployment rules can swap values per stage.
fj: Notebook parameters in a Fabric pipeline are passed as pipeline environment variables and automatically override all Python variables in the notebook.
fs: Only the cell tagged with the `parameters` tag in the notebook receives overrides from the pipeline's Notebook activity. Untagged cells are not affected by pipeline parameter injection, so you must explicitly tag the parameters cell and use those variable names throughout the notebook.

code.label: Notebook parameters cell (tagged 'parameters')
lines: # This cell must be tagged 'parameters' in the notebook metadata
# Pipeline overrides these defaults at runtime

code.label: Pipeline dynamic expression for date-partitioned path

q: What patterns should you use when designing a loading strategy for streaming data into Microsoft Fabric?
a: Microsoft Fabric supports two primary streaming ingestion paths. For high-throughput, low-latency streaming into an Eventhouse (KQL database), the preferred path is Eventstream → Eventhouse, using Fabric's managed event routing with sub-second latency and automatic batching. For streaming into a Lakehouse Delta table, you use Spark Structured Streaming in a long-running notebook job that reads from an Eventstream Kafka-compatible endpoint or an Event Hub connection and writes using `writeStream` with `outputMode` 'append' and a checkpoint location in OneLake. Loading pattern considerations include: checkpointing (Structured Streaming requires a durable checkpoint directory to recover from failures), trigger intervals (micro-batch trigger every 30–60 seconds for near-real-time; `Trigger.AvailableNow()` for scheduled burst loads), watermarking for late data (`withWatermark` on the event timestamp to bound stateful aggregation state), and partitioning the output by event date for query efficiency. For the medallion architecture, land raw events in Bronze (Delta append-only), apply deduplication and parsing in a Silver Structured Streaming job, and use a scheduled batch notebook for Gold aggregations.
fj: Spark Structured Streaming in Fabric writes each incoming event as an individual small Parquet file, requiring frequent OPTIMIZE runs to maintain query performance.
fs: Structured Streaming with Delta Lake uses micro-batch commits that write reasonably sized checkpoint files, not one file per event. You still need periodic OPTIMIZE + VACUUM maintenance, but the write amplification is much lower than one-file-per-event. You control file size via `spark.sql.files.maxRecordsPerFile` and by tuning micro-batch trigger intervals.

code.label: Structured Streaming into Lakehouse Delta table
lines: from pyspark.sql import functions as F

q: How do you choose between a Lakehouse, Warehouse, KQL database, and other Fabric stores for a given analytics workload?
a: Microsoft Fabric offers four primary analytical stores. A Lakehouse stores data as open-format Delta Parquet files in OneLake, exposing a SQL analytics endpoint for T-SQL reads and a Spark engine for full read-write. Choose a Lakehouse when your team uses PySpark or Python, when you need schema evolution and time travel, when your data volumes are very large (petabytes), or when you are building a medallion architecture. A Warehouse is a fully managed T-SQL engine with transaction support, referential integrity (foreign key constraints), and query result caching; choose it when your consumers are SQL-first BI developers, when you need DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) transactions with ACID guarantees, or when you need fine-grained T-SQL security. A KQL database (Eventhouse) is optimized for time-series, log, and telemetry data with sub-second query latency; choose it when ingesting high-volume streaming events, monitoring/observability data, or when KQL's built-in time-series and anomaly functions provide value. A semantic model (Power BI) is the store for BI aggregations and DAX calculations, optimized for interactive dashboard queries. For mixed workloads, a Lakehouse with a SQL analytics endpoint often serves both Spark and SQL consumers without duplication.
fj: A Fabric Warehouse and a Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint are functionally identical for all T-SQL workloads because both expose a SQL endpoint over Delta tables.
fs: The Warehouse supports full DML transactions (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) with ACID semantics, table-level constraints, and query result caching. The Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint is read-only for T-SQL—it does not support DML or DDL writes via T-SQL. Writes to a Lakehouse must go through Spark or the Lakehouse Files API.

q: How do you choose between Dataflow Gen2, notebooks, KQL, and T-SQL for data transformation in Microsoft Fabric?
a: The choice depends on the transformation type, data volume, consumer skill set, and latency requirements. Dataflow Gen2 (Power Query M) is best for structured, multi-source extraction with query folding, light transformations, and no-code accessibility; it suits analysts comfortable with Excel/Power Query who need incremental refresh without writing code. Notebooks with PySpark or Spark SQL handle large-volume complex transformations—nested JSON parsing, ML feature engineering, graph processing, or anything exceeding Power Query limits—and give full programmatic control. KQL (Kusto Query Language) in an Eventhouse is ideal for time-series, log analysis, anomaly detection, and near-real-time transformations on streaming data; its built-in `make-series`, `series_decompose`, and `summarize` operators are far more expressive than T-SQL for temporal patterns. T-SQL in the Warehouse is the right choice when consumers are SQL-first and the transformation logic fits relational patterns—aggregations, joins, window functions—and when you need transactional writes (INSERT/UPDATE/MERGE) with ACID guarantees. The Fabric decision guide recommends: use Dataflow Gen2 as the default for structured ETL; escalate to Spark notebooks for scale or complexity; use KQL for real-time and time-series; use T-SQL for warehouse-native logic.
fj: KQL and T-SQL are interchangeable in Microsoft Fabric because the Eventhouse supports both query languages natively.
fs: KQL databases (Eventhouses) only support KQL and a limited subset of T-SQL for compatibility. They do not support full ANSI T-SQL DML or DDL. T-SQL transformations require a Fabric Warehouse or Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint. Choosing KQL for relational transformation work (complex multi-table joins with transactions) would require rewriting queries entirely.

q: How do OneLake shortcuts work, and what are the steps to create and manage them?
a: OneLake shortcuts are logical pointers that make external data appear as if it lives natively in OneLake, without copying it. Supported external locations include ADLS Gen2, Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, on-premises data via a gateway (preview), and other OneLake paths (cross-workspace shortcuts). To create a shortcut: open a Lakehouse, navigate to the Tables or Files section, right-click → New shortcut, select the source type, provide the connection (URL, credentials or managed identity), and specify the subfolder path. Once created, the shortcut appears as a folder in OneLake and can be queried through the SQL analytics endpoint (for Delta-formatted shortcuts under Tables), via Spark, or via the OneLake File Explorer. Managing shortcuts includes: renaming (not supported—delete and recreate), monitoring access (the shortcut inherits source permissions in passthrough mode for internal OneLake-to-OneLake shortcuts), and setting credentials (stored as a workspace data connection). For security, shortcuts to internal OneLake paths use passthrough mode—the caller's identity is forwarded to the source item, so source-item access controls apply. Shortcuts to ADLS or S3 use stored credentials or managed identity.
fj: OneLake shortcuts copy data from the external source into OneLake during creation so that queries do not depend on network connectivity to the original source.
fs: Shortcuts are pointers—they do not copy data. Queries traverse the shortcut and read directly from the external source at query time. This means query latency depends on source availability and network performance, and there is no local cache. If the source is unavailable, queries on the shortcut will fail.

q: What is mirroring in Microsoft Fabric and how do you configure it for an external database?
a: Mirroring in Microsoft Fabric is a near-real-time replication feature that continuously syncs data from an external database—currently Azure SQL Database, Azure Cosmos DB, Snowflake, or Azure SQL Managed Instance—into OneLake as Delta Parquet files, without requiring a pipeline or ETL. To configure mirroring: go to the Fabric workspace, click New → Mirror, select the source type, provide the connection string and authentication, select the tables to replicate, and specify the target Lakehouse. Fabric then creates a mirrored database item that shows replication status per table, lag metrics, and last sync time. Under the hood, mirroring uses change data capture (CDC) at the source: for Azure SQL it reads the transaction log; for Cosmos DB it reads the change feed. Replicated data lands in the workspace's OneLake as Delta tables and immediately becomes queryable via the SQL analytics endpoint or Spark—no additional steps needed. Mirroring latency is typically seconds to a few minutes. Key constraints: you cannot write back to a mirrored table from Fabric; mirrored tables are read-only in OneLake. Schema changes at the source (ADD COLUMN) are automatically propagated; destructive DDL (DROP TABLE) removes the mirrored table.
fj: Mirroring in Fabric creates a full nightly snapshot of the source database, replacing the previous OneLake copy each day.
fs: Mirroring is continuous, CDC-based replication with near-real-time latency—not a nightly snapshot. It replicates individual row changes as they occur at the source. Full snapshots are only used for the initial seed load; subsequent changes are incremental.

q: When should you choose Eventstream vs. Spark Structured Streaming in Microsoft Fabric for real-time data processing?
a: Eventstream is Fabric's no-code/low-code real-time ingestion and routing service. Choose Eventstream when: you need to fan-out a single stream to multiple destinations simultaneously (Eventhouse, Lakehouse, Reflex, custom endpoint); you want built-in transformations like filtering, aggregation, and windowed grouping via a visual drag-and-drop editor without writing code; or your primary destination is an Eventhouse (KQL database), where the Eventstream-to-Eventhouse connector achieves the lowest latency with managed batching. Eventstream supports Azure Event Hubs, IoT Hub, Kafka endpoints, and Fabric custom app sources. Spark Structured Streaming is the choice when: you need complex programmatic transformations in Python/Scala that exceed Eventstream's built-in operators; you must join a stream with a large static Delta table (stream-static join); you are performing ML inference on each event; or your destination is a Delta table in a Lakehouse with precise control over schema, partitioning, and watermarking. A practical decision rule: if your pipeline is 'ingest → filter/aggregate → store', Eventstream handles it with zero code. If the pipeline involves custom parsing, UDFs, model scoring, or multi-stream joins, use Structured Streaming.
fj: Spark Structured Streaming is always preferable to Eventstream for production streaming because it offers more control over exactly-once delivery semantics.
fs: Eventstream to Eventhouse provides very low-latency ingestion with managed reliability guarantees and requires no cluster management. Structured Streaming requires a running Spark session with its associated capacity cost and startup time. For simple fan-out and aggregation scenarios, Eventstream is both simpler and often more cost-effective. Choose Structured Streaming only when you need programmatic complexity it cannot handle.

q: How do you process data by using Eventstreams in Microsoft Fabric, including routing and transformation operations?
a: An Eventstream item in Fabric is created from New → Eventstream. You start by adding a source—Azure Event Hubs, IoT Hub, a Kafka-compatible endpoint, or a Fabric Custom App (which provides a dedicated Kafka endpoint). After the source is connected, you add processing operators by clicking the '+' between source and destination nodes in the visual designer. Built-in operators include Filter (keep/drop events matching a condition), Aggregate (tumbling, hopping, or session windows with SUM, AVG, COUNT, etc.), Group by, Field selection, Union (merge multiple sources), Expand (flatten nested arrays), and Manage fields (rename or add derived fields). You add one or more destinations: Eventhouse (KQL table), Lakehouse (Delta table), Fabric Activator (for alert-based triggers), or a Custom endpoint. Each destination can optionally receive a filtered or aggregated view of the stream—you branch the topology to send raw events to one destination and aggregated metrics to another. Monitoring is done via the 'Data insights' tab (throughput, latency, event counts) and the 'Runtime logs' tab (warnings, errors, deserialisation failures). A common production pattern: Event Hub → Eventstream with Filter and Aggregate → Eventhouse for dashboard queries + Lakehouse for historical archive.
fj: An Eventstream can only have one destination—to fan out to multiple targets you must create separate Eventstream items for each destination.
fs: A single Eventstream supports multiple simultaneous destinations in a branched topology. You can route raw events to a Lakehouse, aggregated metrics to an Eventhouse, and threshold-based alerts to Fabric Activator—all from one Eventstream item without duplicating the source connection.

q: How do you monitor data ingestion in Microsoft Fabric, and what tools show pipeline and copy activity run status?
a: Microsoft Fabric provides several monitoring surfaces for data ingestion. The primary tool is the Monitor Hub (left navigation → Monitor), which shows a unified list of all pipeline runs, dataflow refreshes, notebook runs, and Spark jobs across the workspace, with status, duration, trigger type, and links to run details. For pipeline runs you can drill into individual activity runs, see inputs/outputs, and access error messages. The Fabric Capacity Metrics app (installable from AppSource) shows capacity consumption per operation type, helping identify when ingestion jobs are throttled or queued. For Copy activity specifically, the run detail shows rows copied, throughput (MB/s), and bytes read/written. Eventstream monitoring uses the built-in Data insights panel (event throughput, source/destination lag, backlog count) and Runtime logs. For Structured Streaming jobs in notebooks, `spark.streams.active` lists running streams and `StreamingQuery.lastProgress` returns micro-batch statistics (input rows, processing rate, trigger timing). Workspace monitoring (preview) provides Eventstream-specific metrics—watermark delay, backlogged events—in a centralized workspace-scoped view.
fj: The only way to monitor Fabric pipeline runs is through Azure Monitor log alerts configured in the Azure portal for the Fabric capacity resource.
fs: The Fabric Monitor Hub in the Fabric portal provides built-in, workspace-scoped visibility into all activity runs without requiring Azure Monitor configuration. Azure Monitor integration is an additional option for advanced alerting and cross-resource dashboards, but it is not the primary or only monitoring tool.

q: How do you monitor data transformation jobs in Microsoft Fabric, including Spark notebook and Dataflow Gen2 runs?
a: Transformation monitoring spans several tools depending on the engine. For Spark notebooks and Spark job definitions, the Monitor Hub lists each run with start time, duration, status, and a link to the Spark UI. The Spark UI (accessed from the run detail) shows stage-level DAG, task-level metrics, executor logs, and shuffle statistics—critical for diagnosing skew and slow tasks. For Dataflow Gen2 refreshes, Monitor Hub shows each refresh attempt, and the refresh detail view presents the query evaluation plan with step-by-step row counts and durations, helping identify which transformation step is the bottleneck. The Fabric Capacity Metrics app shows Dataflow Gen2 CU consumption over time, making it possible to correlate refresh spikes with capacity throttling. For diagnostic logging from within a notebook, use `display(df.explain(mode='formatted'))` to inspect the Spark physical plan and `spark.sparkContext.statusTracker()` for programmatic stage monitoring. In production, instrument notebooks to write a run log record to a Delta table (run_id, start, end, rows_processed, status) so you can query transformation history with SQL.
fj: Dataflow Gen2 refresh failures always surface a detailed step-by-step error message in the Monitor Hub without any additional configuration.
fs: Dataflow Gen2 errors in Monitor Hub may show only a generic error code. For detailed step-level diagnostics you must open the refresh detail, examine the query evaluation plan, and check the error message on the failing step. For query folding failures, the error may appear at the staging Lakehouse layer and require checking the Spark logs of the underlying evaluation.

code.label: Instrument notebook with run log to Delta

q: How do you monitor semantic model refreshes in Microsoft Fabric, and what are common failure causes?
a: Semantic model refresh monitoring in Fabric is available through multiple surfaces. The Fabric portal shows each semantic model's refresh history under the item's '…' → Refresh history, listing each scheduled or manual refresh with start time, duration, and success or failure. For automated pipelines, the Semantic model refresh activity in Data Factory allows you to trigger a model refresh as a pipeline step and capture the outcome, with retry logic if needed. The Fabric Capacity Metrics app tracks refresh operations as background CU usage—refresh spikes that exceed available capacity result in 'Throttled' or 'Queued' states, visible as elevated 'Background CU' bars. Common failure causes include: source credential expiration (connection to a Lakehouse or Warehouse using a specific user account whose token expired), schema drift (a column referenced in a DAX measure was renamed or dropped in the upstream Lakehouse), gateway timeout (for on-premises sources), dataset size exceeding Premium capacity memory limits (resolved by incremental refresh or Large Dataset format), and parallel refresh contention. For pro-active alerting, configure refresh failure notifications in the semantic model settings, which send email alerts to the dataset owner on failure.
fj: Semantic model refresh failures in Fabric are always caused by data quality issues in the upstream Lakehouse tables.
fs: Refresh failures have many possible root causes beyond data quality: expired credentials, schema changes, capacity throttling, gateway issues, and memory limits. Always check the refresh history detail for the specific error type and drill into the Fabric Capacity Metrics app to rule out capacity throttling before investigating data issues.

q: How do you identify and resolve errors in Microsoft Fabric pipelines?
a: Pipeline error investigation starts in the Monitor Hub: select the failed run, open the run detail, and identify which activity failed (the activity node turns red). Click the activity for its error message, input, and output JSON. For Copy activity errors, the output JSON contains `copyDuration`, `rowsRead`, `rowsCopied`, `errors[]` with `Code` and `Message`. Common pipeline errors include: connection failures (invalid credentials or firewall blocking the source endpoint—verify the data connection in Workspace Settings → Data connections), schema mismatch (source has added or removed a column—update the copy mapping), timeout (increase the activity timeout property or add retry with exponential backoff), throttling (upstream system rate-limited the connector—add a delay activity or reduce parallelism with `parallelCopies`), and expression evaluation errors (a dynamic expression resolves to null because a parameter was not passed—validate parameter names match exactly). For notebook activities, the error is typically the Python/Spark exception; click 'See details' to get the stack trace. Add a Fail activity at the end of a failure path to emit a custom error message and mark the pipeline as Failed with a meaningful code, enabling downstream alerting to differentiate error types.
fj: A pipeline failure always indicates a problem with the source data; once the source data is fixed, rerunning the pipeline will succeed.
fs: Pipeline failures are frequently configuration errors—invalid credentials, mismatched schemas, wrong parameter names, throttling limits—unrelated to data quality. Always inspect the activity output JSON and error code first to determine the category of failure before assuming data issues.

q: How do you identify and resolve Dataflow Gen2 errors in Microsoft Fabric?
a: Dataflow Gen2 errors appear in Monitor Hub → Dataflow refreshes. The error detail view shows the query evaluation plan with a red indicator on the failing step. Common error categories: (1) Data source connectivity—invalid credentials or a blocked gateway; resolve by updating the data connection in Workspace Settings and verifying gateway health. (2) Query folding failure—a transformation step cannot be pushed to the source and falls back to in-memory evaluation, which can OOM for large datasets; identify by checking if the step shows 'Cannot be folded' in the Power Query diagnostics, then simplify upstream steps or enable the staging Lakehouse to offload to Spark. (3) Schema change at source—a referenced column was renamed or dropped; open the dataflow in the editor and refresh the schema preview to detect mismatches. (4) Staging Lakehouse write failure—permissions issue on the managed staging workspace; check if the service principal running the dataflow has access. (5) Timeout on large incremental refresh—increase the refresh timeout in the dataflow settings or narrow the incremental refresh window. The Power Query diagnostics pane (View → Diagnostics) in the dataflow editor shows step-level row counts, folding boundaries, and query text sent to the source, which is the most effective tool for performance and error diagnosis.
fj: Enabling the staging Lakehouse in Dataflow Gen2 always fixes query folding errors because all transformations are pushed to the source database.
fs: The staging Lakehouse offloads heavy transformations from the in-memory M engine to Spark—it does not push transformations back to the source. Query folding means transformations are converted to native source queries (SQL, etc.). The staging Lakehouse is the fallback when folding is not possible, processing data in Spark rather than in-memory Power Query.

q: How do you identify and resolve notebook errors in Microsoft Fabric Spark notebooks?
a: Fabric Spark notebook errors appear in the Monitor Hub under Spark job definitions or notebook runs, with a link to the run detail and Spark UI. In the Spark UI, the Stages tab shows which stage failed and the Tasks tab shows individual task failures with exception messages and stack traces. Common Spark error categories: (1) OOM (OutOfMemoryError) on driver or executor—caused by collecting too much data (`df.collect()`, `toPandas()` on large DataFrames); resolve by increasing executor memory in the pool/environment config or rewriting to avoid driver-side collection. (2) AnalysisException (column not found, schema mismatch)—check schema evolution in Delta tables using `DESCRIBE TABLE EXTENDED` or `df.printSchema()`. (3) FileNotFoundException in checkpoint location—the streaming checkpoint was deleted or the OneLake path changed; reset the checkpoint by deleting its directory and restarting the stream from the latest offset. (4) Delta transaction conflict (concurrent write contention)—use `optimisticTransaction` patterns or schedule jobs to avoid overlap. (5) Module import errors—a library is missing in the attached environment; add the library to the workspace environment and reattach. Use `try/except` blocks with structured error logging to Delta for production notebooks, enabling post-run diagnosis without reading raw executor logs.
fj: Increasing the number of Spark executors always resolves notebook OOM errors because it provides more memory across the cluster.
fs: Adding executors increases distributed memory for data partitions but does not help when the OOM is on the driver (e.g., `collect()`, `toPandas()`), because the driver is a single JVM process. Driver OOM requires rewriting the notebook to process data in chunks or use distributed operations instead of pulling to the driver.

code.label: Safe Delta schema inspection to catch column mismatches

q: How do you identify and resolve Eventhouse errors in Microsoft Fabric Real-Time Intelligence?
a: Eventhouse (KQL database) errors surface in several places. For ingestion failures, check the 'Ingestion failures' table using KQL: `.show ingestion failures` lists failed batches with the error message, table name, and timestamp—common causes include schema mismatch between the incoming data and the target table mapping, authentication failure, or the Eventhouse being paused due to capacity. To prevent pausing, enable 'Always On' in the Eventhouse settings (available on F64+ capacities) or set a minimum consumption unit. For query errors, the KQL editor shows inline errors; use `.show queries` to list running and historical queries with their error codes. Eventhouse pausing is the most impactful incident: when a Fabric capacity scales down, Eventhouses without Always-On can be suspended—the Eventstream continues buffering, but backlog grows. Resolution: re-enable the Eventhouse, confirm the Eventstream backlog metric is decreasing (visible in Eventstream Data insights). For data loss investigation, use the `Extent()` function to check ingestion extents and verify row counts match expected event counts. For monitoring at scale, export `.show ingestion failures` results to a Lakehouse table via a scheduled KQL query or pipeline for long-term alerting.
fj: When an Eventhouse is paused, all ingested data from that period is permanently lost because Eventstream does not buffer events.
fs: Eventstream buffers incoming events when the destination Eventhouse is paused. The backlog grows until the Eventhouse resumes, at which point buffered events are ingested. However, if the buffer exceeds the retention period of the source (e.g., Event Hubs default 1–7 day retention), older events will be lost—so prompt recovery is essential.

code.label: Inspect ingestion failures in Eventhouse
lines: | project FailedOn, Table, Database, ErrorCode, Details
// Check row count per table for completeness

q: How do you identify and resolve Eventstream errors in Microsoft Fabric, including backlog and processing failures?
a: Eventstream errors are monitored from two tabs in the Eventstream editor. The 'Data insights' tab provides real-time charts of input event rate, output event rate, consumer lag (backlog), and processing latency—use these to detect when the downstream destination is slower than the source, causing backlog growth. The 'Runtime logs' tab shows structured log entries at three severity levels: Information (normal operational messages), Warning (recoverable issues like temporary source unavailability), and Error (failed event processing, deserialization errors, destination write failures). Common error scenarios: (1) Deserialization error—incoming events do not match the declared input schema (e.g., an Avro field renamed); fix by updating the Eventstream source schema definition. (2) Destination write failure—Eventhouse is paused, Lakehouse permissions changed, or output table was deleted; check destination availability and credentials. (3) High processing latency from operator overload—a windowed aggregation operator is processing too slowly due to high event rate; scale by adjusting the aggregation window size or simplifying the operator graph. (4) Source disconnection—the Event Hub namespace was scaled down; check the Event Hub namespace throughput units. Backlog resolution: if the destination catches up within a few minutes, the issue was transient; if backlog grows continuously, the root cause is persistent throughput mismatch or a destination error.
fj: Eventstream processing errors automatically trigger a replay of failed events from the beginning of the Event Hub retention window.
fs: Eventstream does not automatically replay events from the beginning on error. It resumes processing from the current offset after recovery. To replay historical events you must manually reset the Eventstream source offset to an earlier position, or re-ingest from the Event Hub using a pipeline or notebook.

q: How do you identify and resolve T-SQL errors in a Microsoft Fabric Warehouse?
a: T-SQL errors in the Fabric Warehouse surface directly in the SQL query editor with a standard error message and severity code. The Monitor Hub also shows failed warehouse query activity. Common T-SQL error categories: (1) Permission errors (PERMISSION_DENIED)—the caller lacks SELECT, INSERT, or EXECUTE on the object; resolve with `GRANT <permission> ON <object> TO <principal>`. (2) Object not found (INVALID_OBJECT_NAME)—the table or view was dropped, renamed, or lives in a different schema; use `sys.objects` to verify. (3) Data type mismatch—an INSERT or CTAS is casting a VARCHAR to a numeric type; inspect the source column type with `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS`. (4) Deadlock—two concurrent transactions lock resources in opposite order; redesign to access objects in consistent order or use shorter transactions. (5) Row size exceeded—a row in an INSERT violates the Warehouse's maximum row size; identify wide columns and evaluate VARCHAR(MAX) usage. For DML errors in pipelines, the Stored Procedure activity output contains the error code and message. Use `TRY...CATCH` blocks in stored procedures to capture errors and write them to an `errors` log table, ensuring failures are observable without requiring live monitoring.
fj: T-SQL errors in the Fabric Warehouse can be diagnosed entirely from the Azure portal diagnostic logs without using the Monitor Hub.
fs: The primary diagnostic tool for Fabric Warehouse T-SQL errors is the Monitor Hub and the query editor in the Fabric portal. Azure portal diagnostic logs capture capacity-level metrics but do not show individual SQL query errors or row-level error details—for those you need the Fabric-native monitoring surfaces.

code.label: TRY...CATCH error logging in a stored procedure
lines: THROW;  -- re-raise so the pipeline activity marks as failed

q: How do you identify and resolve OneLake shortcut errors in Microsoft Fabric?
a: OneLake shortcut errors appear when a query traverses a shortcut and fails to reach the source. Symptoms include 'Access Denied', 'Resource Not Found', or 'Network Timeout' when reading from a Lakehouse path that contains a shortcut. Diagnostic steps: (1) Verify the shortcut configuration—open the Lakehouse, right-click the shortcut folder → Properties, and confirm the target path and connection are correct. (2) Check the data connection credentials—for ADLS Gen2 or S3 shortcuts, the stored credentials may have expired; update them in Workspace Settings → Data connections. (3) For internal OneLake shortcuts (cross-workspace), the caller must have at least ReadAll on the source item; check that the requesting user's identity has access to the source workspace item. (4) Verify the source path exists—if the source Lakehouse was deleted or the folder was renamed, the shortcut points to a non-existent location; delete and recreate the shortcut with the correct path. (5) For S3/ADLS shortcuts accessed via Spark, check Spark logs for the underlying ABFS or S3 error code (HTTP 403, 404). Workspace monitoring logs shortcut access failures—filter the workspace monitoring data by resource type 'Shortcut' to see access error patterns.
fj: Shortcut access errors are always caused by the source data moving to a different path, requiring the shortcut to be deleted and recreated.
fs: Shortcut errors have multiple causes: expired credentials, missing permissions on the source item, network/firewall issues, or source path deletion. Always check the data connection credentials and caller permissions before assuming the source path changed, as path changes are less common than credential expiry.

q: How do you optimize a Lakehouse Delta table in Microsoft Fabric using partitioning, V-Order, and the OPTIMIZE command?
a: Delta table optimization in Fabric Lakehouse uses three complementary mechanisms. Partitioning subdivides the table into subdirectories by a column value (commonly a date key), so queries with a partition filter skip entire partitions—effective when cardinality is low (dates, status codes). Avoid high-cardinality partition columns (e.g., user_id) as they create millions of tiny folders. V-Order is a Fabric-specific Parquet write-time optimization that sorts and encodes column data for maximum read efficiency on the Fabric engines (SQL analytics endpoint, DirectLake, Spark); it is enabled by default for tables written by Fabric and improves scan performance by 10–50% in benchmarks. The OPTIMIZE command compacts small Parquet files into larger ones (target ~1 GB each), reducing the small-file problem that causes slow reads. Run `OPTIMIZE <table> ZORDER BY (<col>)` to co-locate related rows physically, which accelerates equality and range filters on the Z-ORDER columns. For large tables, scope OPTIMIZE to a recent partition: `OPTIMIZE <table> WHERE event_date >= '2026-06-01'`. Combine with VACUUM to remove stale Delta log files older than the retention window (default 7 days). In Fabric, the Lakehouse table maintenance feature in pipelines automates OPTIMIZE and VACUUM on a schedule.
fj: Partitioning a Delta table by a high-cardinality column like user_id is recommended because it maximizes partition pruning and query performance.
fs: High-cardinality partitioning creates millions of tiny directories, each with small Parquet files, causing slow file listing, metadata overhead, and increased OPTIMIZE time. Best practice is to partition by low-cardinality columns (date, region, status) and use Z-ORDER for high-cardinality filter columns instead.

code.label: OPTIMIZE with Z-ORDER scoped to recent partition
lines: -- Compact files and co-locate by customer_id within the last 7 days
-- Remove old Delta log files beyond retention window

q: How do you optimize a Microsoft Fabric pipeline for throughput, and what settings control Copy activity performance?
a: Pipeline optimization in Fabric focuses on the Copy activity, which is the primary data movement engine. Key settings: (1) 'Degree of copy parallelism' (parallelCopies)—controls how many parallel read streams and write streams are launched; increase for large tables on sources that support partitioned reads (e.g., SQL table with a partition column or a range). (2) 'Data integration units' (DIU)—a combination of CPU, memory, and network I/O assigned to the copy; higher DIU increases throughput up to the source/destination rate limit. (3) Staged copy—for moves between unsupported source-sink pairs, use an intermediate ADLS Gen2 or Lakehouse staging; staged copy uses the PolyBase or COPY INTO bulk-load path for the destination write, which is far faster than row-by-row INSERT. (4) Enable 'Fast copy' in Dataflow Gen2 for applicable sources to use the COPY INTO path. For pipeline topology, replace sequential ForEach-with-Copy-inside loops with a parallelized ForEach (set batch count) to copy multiple tables concurrently. Add conditional retry logic (up to 3 retries with exponential backoff) for transient network errors. Monitor throughput from the Copy activity output JSON and the Monitor Hub—if MB/s is below expectation, increase DIU and check source throttling metrics.
fj: Increasing the Data Integration Units (DIU) on a Copy activity always linearly doubles the throughput because DIU directly maps to network bandwidth.
fs: DIU increases do not guarantee linear throughput gains because the bottleneck may be at the source (rate limits, index scans) or the destination (write throughput). After a certain DIU level, the copy is bottlenecked by source read speed or destination write capacity. Profile the copy activity output to identify whether the bottleneck is on the read or write side before adding more DIU.

q: How do you optimize a Microsoft Fabric Warehouse for query performance?
a: Fabric Warehouse query optimization uses several techniques. Statistics: the warehouse automatically creates and updates column statistics for tables, but for freshly loaded data you can manually run `UPDATE STATISTICS <table>` to force a rebuild and help the query optimizer choose better plans. Result set caching: the warehouse caches identical query results automatically; queries that hit cache return in milliseconds. Materialized views and user-defined T-SQL views can pre-aggregate common aggregation patterns, reducing query fan-out on large fact tables. Columnstore compression: all Warehouse tables use clustered columnstore index storage by default, which provides optimal scan performance for analytical queries—ensure INSERT operations produce row groups of 1 million+ rows for maximum compression by loading in large batches rather than many small transactions. Table distribution: Fabric Warehouse uses round-robin distribution; for very large join-heavy workloads, consider rewriting joins to use the SQL analytics endpoint of a Lakehouse with Z-ORDER on join keys. Query concurrency: Fabric Warehouse auto-scales concurrency based on capacity SKU; if concurrent queries are queuing, consider scheduling heavy batch jobs during off-peak hours or upgrading capacity. Use the Fabric Capacity Metrics app to identify which warehouse queries consume the most CUs, then focus optimization on those hot paths.
fj: Creating a clustered index on the primary key column of a Fabric Warehouse fact table is the best way to optimize star schema join performance.
fs: Fabric Warehouse uses distributed columnar storage—it does not support traditional B-tree clustered indexes on user tables. All tables are stored as columnstore by default. Adding a B-tree index is not a valid optimization option; instead, use statistics updates, result set caching, materialized aggregation views, and appropriate batch sizes for loads.

code.label: Update statistics and check query cache hit
lines: -- Check if query was served from result cache

q: How do you optimize Eventstreams and Eventhouses in Microsoft Fabric for high-throughput real-time workloads?
a: Eventstream optimization focuses on reducing processing latency and preventing backlog growth. Simplify the operator graph—every Filter, Aggregate, or Expand operator adds processing overhead; remove unnecessary operators and push aggregation to the Eventhouse using KQL materialized views instead of doing it in the Eventstream itself. For high-event-rate sources, use direct ingestion mode (Eventstream → Eventhouse without processing operators) for the lowest latency path and apply transformations in KQL update policies at the Eventhouse level. Eventhouse optimization uses several mechanisms: ingestion batching policy (`.alter table <t> policy ingestionbatching`) controls how long the engine waits before committing an extent—reducing MaximumBatchingTimeSpan from 5 minutes to 30 seconds decreases latency at the cost of more small extents; run periodic `.optimize extent merge` to consolidate extents. For query performance, create materialized views with `.create materialized-view` to pre-aggregate hot dashboard queries—the engine maintains the view incrementally. Caching policy: KQL caches hot data in SSD ('hot cache'); set the hot cache policy to cover the query window your dashboards use (e.g., last 7 days) with `.alter table <t> policy caching hot = 7d`. For the Eventhouse capacity, enable 'Minimum consumption' to prevent cold-start latency on bursty workloads.
fj: The best way to optimize an Eventhouse for real-time dashboard queries is to increase the hot cache window to 365 days so all data is always in SSD cache.
fs: A 365-day hot cache policy caches a full year of data in SSD, which is prohibitively expensive in capacity units and storage costs for most use cases. Hot cache should cover the query window of your dashboards (typically 7–30 days) and no more. Data outside the hot window is served from cold storage with slightly higher latency but at significantly lower cost.

code.label: Set hot cache policy and batching policy on Eventhouse table
lines: // Set hot cache to last 7 days for a telemetry table
// Reduce batching latency to 30 seconds (default is 5 minutes)
// Create materialized view for hourly aggregation
.create materialized-view with (backfill=true) hourly_metrics on table telemetry_raw
